posts.caption:
Leaving stress and returning to the same situation will prolong a cycle. Modify what you can in your daily life. 

#nedranuggets

embedded_comments.text: I was getting to burnout and realized I was doing ALOT of work on my own and it dawned on me I needed to hire an agency to offload the work so 1) I could get more done 2) help me be less stressed. It also made me realize that everyone else was using an agency to help them get things done, whilst I was being superwomen. Lesson learned.

As a teacher who was working with children who were designated as an emotionally disturbed, I went to learn from you and honestly I learned so much about myself that aided me in helping others. You are a blessing 🙌🏻🫶🏻
👏🏽 let’s COmmit to eliminating stress at the root, not just learning to live with it. We deserve peace, not just COping strategies!
This hits deep, self-care is how we thrive. @peacefulpower101
YES 🙌
@selfloveempowerment.co

posts.caption:
#AbbViePartner I’m partnering with @AbbVie on Love in Mind, an educational initiative to elevate the conversation about the emotional impact of living with migraine, beyond the physical symptoms, focusing on romantic relationships. The Love in Mind survey revealed that intimacy can be a real challenge for people navigating migraine in relationships. 
 
But here’s what I know as a licensed relationship therapist: connection can be as simple as being present together. Learn more at LoveInMind.com. 
 
For U.S. Audiences Only

embedded_comments.text: My kids age range is 6-20. They definitely got different parents in the same house.
Totally agree.
Biased therapists need to be held accountable. Encouraging a biased narrative against unknown parents is unethical. Nuance is essential.

replies.text: @unerased.mom she has a video addressing this. Educate yourself.
@unerased.mom Why, what in this video or other content is not correct? She is not diagnosing parents, she is explaining behavior.

embedded_comments.text: But also parents change over time

I’m now in the phase of my sibling trying to gaslight me about our upbringing and relationship. Which would probably work better if I magically didn’t have a memory. And hadn’t kept a journal since I was 12.
Nah it’s probably because I’m a “drama queen” 😭😂🤣😂🤣😂🤣😂🤣😂
@unerased.mom what is a biased therapist?

There’s a nuance that someone else was getting at as well. It’s not just curiosity but an openness to who you are. My parents want to know a lot about me, but it’s about access to me not genuinely caring about who I am or what my life is about.
They don’t want to be curious though.

My experience of that “full rejection” of us as an autistic/ADHD family is unfortunately common. Anecdotally, a lot of neurodivergent families end up estranged from the grandparent generation. Not through choice, but through necessity. Curiosity is absent, while judgement and condemnation is the norm. It’s impossible to have relationships with grandparents who see their grandkids’ struggles and differences as a threat to their image and ego.
Sometimes, if they're not showing curiosity, ITS A BLESSING. specially in disfunctional  families, they put you down just bcz you're not following same path
Awkward for anyone who mistook access for love
Facts!!!

Sure and what about changes like - I don’t live in my home state, I’m not married to some one of the same race or religion, I’m living in a city with a hcol, not the countryside, we don’t drink or use drugs, we don’t even speak the same language at home anymore…
What if they don’t show any curiosity about your life?

posts.caption:
NEW episode of @thecallinghomepodcast with @emma.in.therapy out now!! We’re talking about the show Shameless and narcissistic parents, sibling dynamics, parentification, dysfunctional families, and what happens when a crisis only makes things worse.

Comment POD on this post and I’ll DM you the link to listen!

embedded_comments.text: POD
My dads wife died of cancer and I think he got worse - more lies, more gaslighting and more deception to get needs met. Sad.
Or what about wishing my mother would die becuase then everything would be easier.

I thought when I was diagnosed as autistic aged 42 that it would force my parents to treat me differently. To see that their ever-unattainable standards for how I was supposed to be and live were unreasonable given my neurodivergent wiring, and be more accepting of me as I am.

It had the opposite effect. They got angry with me for being diagnosed (an autistic daughter isn’t great for the family image), and used it as an opportunity to double down on talking to me like I was inherently inferior to them.
Sent you a message!

embedded_comments.text: How much is the app?

Right now, Iran is disconnected from the world.
The digital blackout is hiding violence against people.
Awareness saves lives. Please help amplify these voices and raise awareness. ✌️🙏💕

#DigitalBlackoutIran #HumanRightsIran #StandWithIran
This is really special and needed! 💓💓💓
I LOVE this so much
Thank you so much 🙏. I would love to join 🥹.
How do I join?
This is AMAZING!!! 🤩
I love this 💙
This pivot is SPECIAL.

One of the final steps for me to finally let go and grieve and stop blaming myself was the out of nowhere thought that my parents would never want me to be in a relationship with a man or a friend who treated me the way they did. Game changer.

I treat my adult children like my cherished friends.  I respect them, their spouses, children and the lives they live.  We have a great relationship because I mind my own business and always here to listen when they want to talk.  It’s not a hard concept to follow.

I see how that could be taken the wrong way cause yeah, we are not peers. However I also see in my experience that my parent treats their friends better - at least in terms of prioritizing spending time with them, respecting their time/planning ahead to make sure they see each other regularly, and just speaking with more charity to them and checking in to ask how they are doing, etc

How about if we treated our underdeveloped small children the same way- as young PEOPLE who are struggling because they don’t have the developmental capacity- the amount I hear things like “shirty teenagers” or “spoiled kids” from those parents is… startling

Especially if your kids are fully independent and no longer financially tied to their parents they should be treated with love and respect - just like the parents treat their friends - or anyone they want to keep around. You need to cultivate a new type of relationship with them. Don't offer unsolicited advice or criticize them unnecessary.  That is exactly how lose your kids.
@jennifermarie.nyc I am a boomer, this is definitely talking about my parents

@allifitzpatrick yes I’ve been thinking that as well. It feels to me sometimes that when we heal and resist this, it’s like we’re fighting fascism at the micro level. Like what we’re seeing is the old system trying to hold on. I don’t see this talked about very often so thank you for naming it!

embedded_comments.text: I just had an on & off relationship for about 3yrs with a covert narcissist. He married a person he had longer history with, they went to college together. Married her a week after my birthday last month.

The victim narrative is a very important aspect of this too + mirroring your empathetic and caring skills at the start of the relationship. Therefore you might think that they are emotionally mature and available, but what they actually do it to mirror your skills in that regard.
💯Definition of my ex @Bill Rehaume 💯💯💯💯💯

I can think of too many ppl like this! Really wish we had not only a more therapy-positive society with better mental health infrastructure, but a society that normalized trying to understand the complexities of the people around us. Not giving a pass, but at least allowing space for context to promote accountability/healing. Restorative justice even!

posts.caption: Honor her by refusing to settle. How do YOU honor your mother? #selfhealers

embedded_comments.text: Aw but my mother doesn't want he to honor her in this way 😂 she wants me to suffer in the same ways she did abiding by "the will of god " she's giddy at that prospect

The best way to honour your Mother is to honour her before you enter relationships, to make sure you've done the work on yourself beforehand, especially if you've had a Father who wasn't the best role model, and definitely if you've had an abusive ex. You need to break the cycle and you can't do that unless you have healed.

my stbx thinks it’s normal to cheat and say “I’m not suppose to dwell on it." who says something like that? and idk how to leave although I truly wanted to leave a long time ago. i told my friend about it and she suggested that i should seek advice from support groups on Facebook where i was recommended to contact ( Spyconsultant1@gmail.com ) on gmail, it was an eye opening experience for me. they helped me gain access into my cheating narc husband's phone call records and retrieved all his social media accounts messages and text messages, both deleted and non-deleted messages,  including messages from his email contacts too. for a long time he has been having an affair with lots of college girls and all those while I have been having this feeling that he has changed completely but I kept doubting, now I know all his promises of  fidelity are all lies, he broke the promise . Now I got lots of proofs to show his a narc, I’m filing for divorce real soon and taking full custody of our children, with the informations I’ve gotten, no one is suppose to go through situation, I advice you contact this cyber worker who helped me, through their details below 
Gmail: Spyconsultant1@gmail.com
YESSS!!!

When I finally understood that my biggest flaw was learned from her about over sacrificing, I was able to finally stop trading love for over giving. I reclaim my voice, my power to choose, my power to set and hold boundaries. Our parents teachings are not always bad, but how deeply we reinforce them can create the same cycle of sickness and unhappiness that we are not meant to live.🔥
Powerful post! What did you mean “ to unsubscribe from marriage an motherhood is self neglect”?
@see_more_glass_ mine too.

posts.caption: Limerence sometimes begins with an emotionally reactive parent as a survival response. A child cannot risk perceiving their parent as unsafe, so the brain learns to selectively hold onto only the positive and dissociate from what feels threatening or scary. This neurological blueprint doesn’t stay in childhood. It travels into adult relationships where red flags feel familiar, predictable, and like “home.” #selfhealers

embedded_comments.text: Omg 🥲 I’ve been doing this my whole life until I found out a year ago something is not adding up in this story. Do you have any advice on how to forgive this parent, or how to deal with the situation? Since I found this out, I've been rejecting my mother.

I’m glad it has a name. But, I noticed that I have a hard time forgiving myself. That there’s also shame there for not seeing the red flags. Hearing this, it helps that it wasn’t just a 2 year lapse of judgement but also an imprinted pattern. I was open to the idea that this ‘healing’ thing is, like inception, ‘planting’ or hyper fixation on the negative memories. Like someone was telling me to remember my mom. Like I wasn’t giving my mom enough Grace and I continue to harp on the lack of protection , lack of feeling seen, but this explains that I really did reach adulthood by blocking those memories.
True
Limerance is so real
Wow. 😬
Mind blown how exact this is. It’s crazy that unhealthy families are so committed to concealing abuse and willfully pass down generational harm to their children 😢
Omg 😳🤯🤯
Yes 🙌
I wasn't raised by my parents really, so I agree w this, but for caregivers in general.
This really hits. So many people are quietly experiencing this.

embedded_comments.text: this
I see all of this in my daughter in-law for sure i feel for my grandbabies. They ha e to deal with her everyday and they cry to be with their dad. 😡🙏🙏🙏
Damn
The house I grew up in 🙌. A place where fear and rules dominated, courtesy of my unyielding father. No love was shown, just expectations.

I’ve recently gone no contact after my narcissistic father sent abusive messages as I wasn’t following his orders. He did this whilst I was 7months pregnant and caused me a lot of distress as the messages were awful. I’ve decided that I don’t want to have any contact with him and I don’t want my baby to meet him. My husband thinks our child has a right to meet their grandfather, and that I’m weaponising our child and that our child would hold a grudge when older if they never met him. But I see it as breaking the cycle and stopping my child from ever having to experience the abuse I did. I have said when our child is old enough to understand they can make that decision but I want to protect them from his behaviour. What should I do 🥺

This was my dad. We recently had his funeral and his Co-Workers showed up with tons of praise reports calling him their work-dad and recounting the endless times he told them that he was proud of them and he was there for all their milestones… Things me and my siblings never heard or experienced. Smh😢 I miss him though. #traumabond

@danahrita this is my dad to a T. He’s the “good time Charlie” to everyone on the outside but I spent my childhood tip toeing on eggshells to not set him off. He constantly emotionally abused my mom calling her a fat, lazy, pos slob, etc. They would fight and scream and then he wouldn’t speak to anyone for a few days and just be an aggressive drunk until he decided the fight was over and they’d go back to being “in love” for a few days until they repeated it again. And again. And again. I had my own kids and asked that he not speak to my mom that way in front of them, I don’t think that was an unheard of request. I dated some really bad guys over the years because in my mind, that behavior was normal. It’s the way my dad always treated my mom and they’d been married for 25+ years so that’s just how marriage is, right? Wrong. But I didn’t know that until I met my husband. 8 years in and I still catch myself apologizing for things I shouldn’t because I expect him to react like my dad would, I don’t want that for my kids. Instead of speaking to my mom respectfully in front of my kids, he began talking to me the way he talks to her, his way of asserting him dominance. My mom enables him and says “that’s just the way he is”. They’re too immature to take accountability, apologize or remotely change their behavior so we haven’t spoken to them in 9 months. Now everyone is coming at me because “he’s such a nice guy” and I’m weaponizing their grandchildren by keeping them away. No, I’m protecting my kids the way no one ever protected me.
@jenise318 I wish my mom had left. Staying with my dad really messed me up.

posts.caption:
Express your anger, your immune system will thank you. You are NOT a child who’s going to get in trouble. Expressing your anger is healthcare. 

Have you ever gotten sick from not expressing yourself? Or for holding something in for too long?

embedded_comments.text: What if the other person is a narcissist?
What important message! We can do something different!
Yuuuuuuuup
At my job, yes I am... They can fire me. Most time I freeze and don't process it until 4 hours later.
Except for when you express your anger/hurt/feelings in a respectful way to grown adults, they DARVO you and make themselves the victim 🙄
Yes!
@itsmshayes41

What strikes me about this is how many people are walking around thinking their survival responses are personality traits. The over-explainer. The people pleaser. The one who can’t sit still. None of that is who they are it’s who they had to become  in defense.
“It is impacting your immune system, your thyroid…”

posts.caption: Ask yourself: is this worth spiking my cortisol? #selfhealers

embedded_comments.text: Ugh. Spot on.  You are validating my front row seat observation.  It sometimes feels heartbreaking to face this truth and how they live in a loveless world. Still, it’s zero contact for me.
Mannnn its so exhausting!!!!! They just won't leave you alone

This question should be on every manager's desk. The cortisol spike from chronic workplace stress is one of the most underdiagnosed risks in organizations today. Brazilian labor law (NR1) is now requiring companies to map psychosocial risk — and this is exactly why: unchecked stress is costing lives and billions. Thank you for this reminder, Dr. Nicole 🙏

This question is everything. 🧠 In organizational neuroscience, chronic cortisol spikes from toxic work environments are now legally recognized as psychosocial risk factors in Brazil's new workplace mental health law (NR1 Psicossocial, effective May 2026). What was once just "bad management" is now a measurable, auditable health hazard. The body keeps the score — and now, so does the law. Thank you for this reminder, Dr. Nicole 🙏
Sad but true…I live with this daily unfortunately.

posts.caption:
Next month inside @selfhealerscircle is dedicated entirely to covert narcissism.

🫀Guest Speakers
📕 Expert Authors
🗣️ Exclusive Content

This is the education most people never get and it changes everything. We’ve had more requests for this topic than any other topic and know spaces will fill up fast.

posts.caption:
As alcohol consumption has dropped (a great thing!), people are starting to push back now and claim we should be drinking more!

AKA this is because similar to tobacco, alcohol is a big money maker and they have lobbyists, who are trying to prevent these companies from losing money. 

Alcohol does NOT help you calm down long term. It does not regulate your anxiety. You feel temporarily better and have rebound effects (even if you don't feel it). For more, check out my book, NOT DRINKING TONIGHT anywhere books are sold.

I have always been of the opinion, drink or not it's your choice. But I believe we deserve to have the facts about how alcohol impacts your brain and body. Current research shows that there is no "safe amount of alcohol to consume." It poses health risks. This doesn't mean you must not drink, but you should be aware.

Alcohol is a scam and you can still drink if you want! What do you think?

embedded_comments.text: Would you say this is also true with just 1 drink (ie outside of binge drinking/partying)? I love a microbrew on tap 😢
This is some of my favorite psychoeducation for my clients who are considering cutting back or stopping entirely!
I am 7 months sober today. Informative videos like this keep me going ✨🩷
I reached 10 months alcohol free two days ago.  Best decision I’ve ever made.
I stopped drinking because it stopped giving me whatever relaxation even before the hangover hit
I also wonder if it’s because antidepressants are also more commonly described and they’re told not to drink on them
I agree. Also, credit cards
Check out my book NOT DRINKING TONIGHT for a non preachy guide to re-evaluating your relationship with alcohol
I agree I completely removed alcohol from my life

posts.caption:
I’m a therapist who thinks the oversimplification of mental health content on the internet is hurting us!!

Here are some things I believe as a therapist that might surprise you: 

Not every uncomfortable feeling is a sign something is wrong.

I think we’ve made “healing” sound like a destination and it’s setting everyone up to feel broken forever.

Nothing is rarely 100% good or bad. There is nuance to almost everything. But the internet flattens things and makes it hard to see. And I fear the more time we spend here, the harder it is to tolerate what real complex relationships feel like. 

The secret sauce to most relationships is REPAIR. And this is something your therapist should be supporting you with. 

Follow me for more realistic and nuanced advice! Or listen to the Nuance Needed podcast!

embedded_comments.text: I really appreciate the emphasis on repair and complexity here 👏
Yes. Yes. Yes PREACH

I absolutely agree with this! Context and nuance is so important and the downside of short form content is that it doesn’t allow much space for them. I really struggle with this when creating my own content - always including a disclaimer that therapeutic topics are very nuanced and none of my posts will apply to everyone and to every situation.
Yes to repair! Without repair, relationships can't be authentic and/or safe. The book The Power of Discord was hugely impactful for me when I read it during the pandemic - highly recommend!
So much this. Nuance is so important🔥

- the myth that we only need one person 
- and SO much more! 

Listen to podcast and be sure to check out Nedra’s incredible new book, The Balancing Act! Out everywhere books are sold !

posts.caption:
To be clear— this is a cheeky video and a trend I saw. These things my therapist said to me were transformative!!

What’s the most painful but helpful thing your therapist has said to you?

embedded_comments.text: I don't see the issue

She called me out for my inability to take compliments 💀 she said something along the lines of “compliments are uncomfortable for you because you expect people to talk to you the way you talk to yourself.” 
She done got me 😂
All of these are correct 💯✅
She got your ass 😂😭
Did you know that others can be burdened by your love?😂
Shame and empathy cant coexist. If you want your husband to have empathy for you, you can support that by not shaming him
What when you have been SAd? I just want to break their bones.

None of these things are horrible, just hard to hear and also are questions to build self awareness, not passing judgment. The therapist is trying to get you to actually break patterns and be more thoughtful, improving yourself is ultimately the hard work of therapy. When she says you're hurt but you can work on things and take accountability for your part, she's encouraging you to be more emotionally mature and responsible, not justifying the hurt caused and actions of people not in session with you.  When they tell you you can change the outcome or work from where things are even if you didn't cause it, she's giving you agency instead of just being a victim, which will make you stronger even when people hurt you. Mutual accountability is present in most conflicts: it's a good thing when you recognize that both sides have done hurtful things and owning your part in it because it's almost never a villain you're dealing with, but another flawed person and you can't control them but you can work on yourself and better ways to deal with conflict. Discussing your boundaries is also helpful, to discuss if they're coming from a place of empathy, need and reasonableness as well as consideration for the people you deal with and those relationships , not just a way to control the situation. She may realize discussing those boundaries that the boundaries you set are reasonable and necessary. Congratulations, you have a therapist who trusts you enough to do hard work to improve yourself and build awareness, and asks questions to help you grow.

@iertje.de.boomkikker i agree with you, its not control, i think peole just dont want to walkback that word and explain it away in this contwxt. Yes people pleasing its subliminal strategy to get you what you want, and what you want is safety, but control requires the other person be expending themselves to your end, people pleasing depletes a person often without those they please even being aware.

posts.caption:
5 questions I get pushback from you turds about 

#mentalhealth #therapy #relationshiptips #dating #datingadvice

embedded_comments.text: #4 is SO SMART and kinda just blew my mind a bit, I so lean toward just bringing everything up but can also have maaaajor relationship anxiety but actually asking the person if they'd like me to talk about it had never occured to me before!!
Why would you want to tell your partner that it's ok to cheat and not tell you. That seems like a bad precedent.

Yes to all of these and please know some of these things a person won’t know about themselves until they get there. Or they will feel one way now but once they’re in the moment have totally different feelings. This is not to say these questions shouldn’t be asked. They should. Also, speaking from personal experience, always remember that feelings, opinions, desires, etc. can change.
6. If you ask any of these and he just brushes them away, ask yourself what boundaries you'll have for kindness to yourself

My ex husband never felt guilty after I found out ge cheated throughout our 20 year marriage. And we spoke about that early on the relationship, we agreed to tell the other person, he never did, and always cheated

Would love to know when it makes sense to define the relationship or when it might spoil the vibe or the mood?  How are folks in the modern world approaching the behavior of defining the relationship?

@thelipbalmqueen honestly these things I've discussed with people I'm just starting to date, not as a let's make choices but what kind of relationships I've had and what type of person am I. I think if you've gotten to the relationship stage already and not chatted even casually about this with them before, it's probably a red flag. It doesn't have to be, sit down and ask these specific questions this exact way all at once before saying yes we're in a relationship, but you should have a pretty good idea of what their answer is from the conversations you have had with them and not just imagining what they would say.

posts.caption:
5 science backed reasons it’s so incredibly hard to end a long term relationship.

#mentalhealth #therapy #relationshiptips #dating #breakup

embedded_comments.text: Gosh - the SCIENCE behind all of this almost makes it even more scary. Thank you for explaining this so intelligently. 😍

The grief over losing that part of yourself and the future you thought you had mixed with the depletion of oxytocin is hard to come out of. Also realizing that they might not have been the person you thought they were.

This all makes so much sense. I had to leave an 11 month abusive relationship last year and it felt like one of the hardest things I ever had to do. One I “detoxed”, the clouds lifted and everything felt lighter and made sense again, and I could look back on everything with clear eyes. But I was definitely addicted to the good parts of the relationship, and put up with the shitty parts for the sake of the high.
And you know time won't give me time. And time makes lovers feel. Like they've got something real. But you and me, we know. They've got nothing but time.
This could apply to the empty nest transition as well.
So true. How often when people are in long term conflict they underestimate how the years of attachment will effect them

Just ended a relationship that was with somebody who was supposed to be my fiancé. It’s not easy, the identity you build around someone you trust and cared for isn’t easy. The grief will be there, but the pain will last. But it’ll never be forever. I want to detach but I want to honor the feelings I feel as fresh as they are.
